Mimir Compactor PVC Pressure

Current forensic trace: Grafana event to governed GitOps remediation

A request-by-request forensic trace of a governed Mimir compactor PVC remediation, including identity, operation references, Postgres state, Git recovery, and executable invariants.
Author

Observability Platform Engineering

Published

July 16, 2026

This is a slow-motion trace of one concrete operation. Grafana reports pressure on a Mimir compactor PVC. Kestra sequences typed HTTP calls. The gateway derives identity, obtains a bounded plan, evaluates policy, binds approval, and makes recoverable provider calls. Git remains the production mutation boundary. Postgres, not Kestra or the model, owns durable operation authority.

ImportantCurrent implementation

This edition describes the invariant-kernel implementation at source revision d6c5046. After policy, Kestra no longer copies every prior response into every request. Policy returns a caller-bound operationRef; later routes send that handle, and the gateway reloads the server-owned snapshot from Postgres. The catalog is a compiled, digest-verified runtime artifact, and Git publication recovers by exact semantic readback.

Read this first

The execution has three phases:

Phase What crosses the wire What becomes authoritative
Understand Grafana event, then complete evidence and plan components Typed evidence, plan, policy, and immutable identity
Authorize One compact operationRef Server-owned snapshot, approval binding, preflight binding
Act and prove The same operationRef plus route metadata Outbox state, exact Git objects, provider correlation, Argo readback, audit chain
NoteTwo correction points

The first trigger is the Grafana webhook, not the reconciliation sweep. The sweep only revisits operations already known to the gateway. The model is not an autonomous Kubernetes agent: it is one provider call inside planning and holds no Git, Kubernetes, ITSM, incident, or database credentials.

What runs where

Component Runtime Reads Writes Authority
Grafana alerting Observability plane Metrics and alert rules Webhook delivery Detect and notify
Kestra Automation namespace Trigger body and gateway responses Execution history Sequence and retry tasks
Planner gateway Multi-replica Kubernetes Deployment Compiled catalog, OPA, Postgres, approved APIs Typed state and bounded provider calls Validate, authorize, correlate, recover
Model provider External approved API Sanitized evidence and catalog-bounded prompt Typed plan proposal Propose only
OPA Gateway policy boundary Evidence, plan, catalog, runtime facts Allow or deny Deny unsafe intent
Postgres Managed database, dedicated gateway schema Durable records Attempts, snapshots, outbox, audit, compensation Operation authority and recovery
Git provider Enterprise Git service Base ref and target file Deterministic branch, commit, draft PR Change system of record
Human reviewer Existing pull-request process Evidence and exact diff Review and merge decision Final production authorization
Argo CD Existing GitOps control plane Approved Git revision Kubernetes reconciliation Apply merged desired state

Kestra stores its execution graph and task outputs in the Kestra database. The gateway stores safety-critical state in a separate gateway schema. Kestra can show what task ran; it is not the authority for whether an external effect may run again.

1. Detection and ingress

Grafana routing

Grafana alert rule
  -> notification policy
  -> webhook contact point
  -> Kestra Webhook trigger
  -> catalog_remediation_aiops execution

The contact point uses this URL shape:

https://kestra.example.com/api/v1/main/executions/webhook/
aiops.kestra/catalog_remediation_aiops/<secret-webhook-key>

The canonical flow declares:

triggers:
  - id: alertmanager_webhook
    type: io.kestra.plugin.core.trigger.Webhook
    key: __KESTRA_WEBHOOK_KEY__
    wait: false
    returnOutputs: false

wait: false acknowledges ingress without waiting for planning, approval, Git, or verification. The workflow continues asynchronously.

Exact webhook delivery

One Grafana delivery can contain one alert instance or a notification group. The repository fixture contains one instance:

{
  "version": "4",
  "receiver": "aiops-kestra-webhook",
  "status": "firing",
  "catalogEntryId": "mimir.compactor.pvc-pressure",
  "groupKey": "{}/{alertname=\\"MimirCompactorPVCPressure\\"}:{}",
  "commonLabels": {
    "alertname": "MimirCompactorPVCPressure",
    "cluster": "contextual-cellgroup-a",
    "cell_id": "contextual-cell-a",
    "namespace": "mimir",
    "workload": "mimir-compactor",
    "persistentvolumeclaim": "mimir-compactor-data"
  },
  "alerts": [
    {
      "status": "firing",
      "fingerprint": "grafana-labels-fingerprint-9d8c7b6a",
      "startsAt": "2026-07-01T18:00:00Z",
      "labels": {
        "alertname": "MimirCompactorPVCPressure",
        "cluster": "contextual-cellgroup-a",
        "cell_id": "contextual-cell-a",
        "namespace": "mimir",
        "workload": "mimir-compactor",
        "persistentvolumeclaim": "mimir-compactor-data",
        "severity": "warning"
      },
      "annotations": {
        "summary": "Mimir compactor PVC pressure",
        "description": "Mimir compactor PVC has less than 15 percent free capacity."
      }
    }
  ]
}

The native Grafana fingerprint is evidence about the alert instance. It is not the universal key because it does not encode catalog meaning, repository, requested change, approval, or external operation type.

2. Compiled remediation contract

The reviewed catalog source defines what the condition means and what a safe response may contain:

id: mimir.compactor.pvc-pressure
title: Mimir compactor PVC pressure
component: mimir-compactor
statefulness: stateful
riskRating: medium
triggerSources:
  - grafana.alertmanager
  - scheduled.sweep
signals:
  - alertname: MimirCompactorPVCPressure
  - promql: kubelet_volume_stats_used_bytes / kubelet_volume_stats_capacity_bytes > 0.85
allowedPrActions:
  - update_helm_value
  - increase_pvc_size
  - increase_resource_request
prTarget:
  repository: observability-gitops
  pathTemplate: clusters/{{ cluster }}/namespaces/{{ namespace }}/mimir/values.yaml
  fields:
    - compactor.persistentVolume.size
    - compactor.resources.requests
    - compactor.resources.limits
costControls:
  maxPvcIncreasePercent: 50
  maxPvcSize: 1Ti
approval:
  production: required

Production does not reinterpret YAML on every request. The build compiles all 31 entries into one deterministic artifact:

Artifact Current value
Reviewed source catalog/remediation-catalog.yaml
Compiler scripts/compile_remediation_catalog.py
Runtime bundle catalog/remediation-catalog.compiled.json
Bundle format aiops.remediation-catalog.compiled.v1
Source SHA-256 80b918afb1d5f56b2b12ced0aa09c9ddde66a01e5a262343d837a84ef406a255
Canonical payload digest a906f9333b575ba457054aa146e22d3994a04f9ae3af3125faa71cd13f988c59

3. Canonical execution

Transport and persistence map

Step Kestra request Gateway response Durable effect
1 Raw Grafana event to /v1/evidence Typed evidence Evidence component and identities
2 Evidence to /v1/plan Typed proposal Plan component
3 Evidence + plan to /v1/policy Policy + operationRef Operation snapshot revision 1
4 operationRef to /v1/approval/request Bound approval Attempt, approval, snapshot revision
5 operationRef to /v1/providers/preflight Route admission Preflight and snapshot revision
6 operationRef to /v1/gitops/pr Exact draft PR Outbox, Git objects, snapshot revision
7 operationRef to /v1/providers/notify Fanout result One correlated object per sink
8 operationRef to /v1/argo/verify Revision-bound observation Read-only verification
9 operationRef plus metadata to /v1/audit Audit bundle Latest projection and hash-chained event

The flow has concurrency limit 8 with QUEUE. Kestra owns a small transport retry budget. The gateway and Postgres own provider-attempt eligibility.

Step 1: evidence

1

Normalize the notification

Kestra sends the external event. The gateway selects the catalog, redacts unsafe fields, derives topology and alert lineage, and reads the authoritative current Git value. 

POST /v1/evidence
X-AIOPS-Gateway-Token: [redacted]
Content-Type: application/json
{
  "alert": {
    "version": "4",
    "catalogEntryId": "mimir.compactor.pvc-pressure",
    "alerts": [{
      "fingerprint": "grafana-labels-fingerprint-9d8c7b6a",
      "labels": {
        "cluster": "contextual-cellgroup-a",
        "cell_id": "contextual-cell-a",
        "namespace": "mimir",
        "workload": "mimir-compactor",
        "persistentvolumeclaim": "mimir-compactor-data"
      }
    }]
  }
}

Representative material response:

{
  "catalogEntryId": "mimir.compactor.pvc-pressure",
  "cluster": "contextual-cellgroup-a",
  "cellId": "contextual-cell-a",
  "namespace": "mimir",
  "workload": "mimir-compactor",
  "sensitiveDataIncluded": false,
  "gitopsAuthoritativeCurrent": {
    "repository": "observability-gitops",
    "baseBranch": "main",
    "targetPath": "clusters/contextual-cellgroup-a/namespaces/mimir/mimir/values.yaml",
    "field": "compactor.persistentVolume.size",
    "value": "200Gi"
  },
  "identity": {
    "alertFingerprint": "alert:v1:2cacc6298d7fd96fec6e4296e8dc5794",
    "resourceScopeKey": "remediation:v1:ee5b4d4297b1242f6dfd9b33ec6217b4",
    "caseKey": "case:v1:6b0fa0d60c77c1ac5a9202a01c05b4d3",
    "auditCaseKey": "audit:v1:9c3cff8c38974c415bdad7075e4cbc7b"
  }
}

Step 2: plan

2

Request one bounded proposal

Kestra sends typed evidence. The gateway adds selected catalog context, calls the configured planner, and validates a typed PlanComponent. 

body: |
  {
    "evidence": {{ outputs.collect_evidence.body | jq('.') | first }}
  }
{
  "catalogEntryId": "mimir.compactor.pvc-pressure",
  "provider": "anthropic",
  "llmUsed": true,
  "decision": "propose_gitops_pr",
  "confidence": "medium",
  "proposedChange": {
    "action": "increase_pvc_size",
    "targetRepository": "observability-gitops",
    "targetPath": "clusters/contextual-cellgroup-a/namespaces/mimir/mimir/values.yaml",
    "field": "compactor.persistentVolume.size",
    "currentValue": "200Gi",
    "proposedValue": "300Gi",
    "directMutation": false
  },
  "verification": [
    "PVC utilization falls below 75 percent",
    "Mimir compactor succeeds within 30 minutes",
    "Argo CD reports Synced and Healthy"
  ]
}

The model can propose only. Its response must match the schema and then survive deterministic policy. Model credentials cannot write to Git or Kubernetes.

Step 3: policy and operation reference

3

Evaluate semantics and issue a durable handle

Kestra sends evidence and plan. OPA evaluates the catalog boundary. The gateway persists the initial server-owned snapshot and returns its operation reference. 

body: |
  {
    "evidence": {{ outputs.collect_evidence.body | jq('.') | first }},
    "plan": {{ outputs.planner.body | jq('.') | first }}
  }

Policy checks the kill switch, action allowlist, exact repository/path/field, direct-mutation flag, PVC direction, 50 percent cap, 1 TiB maximum, topology, and sensitive-data boundary.

{
  "catalogEntryId": "mimir.compactor.pvc-pressure",
  "decision": "allow",
  "violations": [],
  "enforcedBoundary": "gitops_pull_request",
  "forbiddenActions": [
    "direct_kubectl_apply",
    "direct_kubectl_patch",
    "direct_kubectl_delete",
    "direct_shell",
    "direct_ssh",
    "shrink_pvc",
    "merge_own_pr"
  ],
  "killSwitchEnabled": false,
  "operationRef": "operation-ref:v1:48619c00cce83a5cb797d3188cdf2225"
}

How the operation reference is created

{
  "attemptKey": "attempt:v1:c6a3967e3d020c213af8697c45487dbf",
  "issuedComponentKey": "audit:v1:9c3cff8c38974c415bdad7075e4cbc7b",
  "schemaVersion": "aiops.operation-snapshot.v1"
}
SHA-256:
48619c00cce83a5cb797d3188cdf222595564faf410c832b9b89bf5f0bb16fd7

Public handle:
operation-ref:v1:48619c00cce83a5cb797d3188cdf2225

The snapshot is stored as:

aiops_state_records
  category        = operationSnapshots
  idempotency_key = operation-ref:v1:48619c00cce83a5cb797d3188cdf2225
{
  "schemaVersion": "aiops.operation-snapshot.v1",
  "operationRef": "operation-ref:v1:48619c00cce83a5cb797d3188cdf2225",
  "attemptKey": "attempt:v1:c6a3967e3d020c213af8697c45487dbf",
  "issuedComponentKey": "audit:v1:9c3cff8c38974c415bdad7075e4cbc7b",
  "ownerCallerId": "shared-secret",
  "initialContextDigest": "[SHA-256 binding]",
  "context": {
    "evidence": {"catalogEntryId": "mimir.compactor.pvc-pressure"},
    "plan": {"decision": "propose_gitops_pr"},
    "policy": {"decision": "allow"}
  },
  "componentDigests": {
    "evidence": "[evidence intent digest]",
    "plan": "[plan intent digest]",
    "policy": "[policy intent digest]"
  },
  "completedRoutes": ["/policy"],
  "revision": 1
}

The canonical flow uses the shared gateway token, so this fixture’s caller is shared-secret. When per-caller authentication is enabled, the gateway instead authenticates X-AIOPS-Caller-Id against that caller’s token and binds the handle to the resulting identity. A caller cannot use another caller’s handle.

ImportantWhat the handle is not

The operation snapshot is a convenience projection, not side-effect authority. It prevents Kestra from resending and substituting prior components. External effect authority still lives in attempt and outbox records plus exact provider readback.

Step 4: approval

4

Bind a human decision to one immutable intent

Kestra sends only the handle. The gateway reloads evidence, plan, and policy from Postgres, requires policy to be complete, and issues or replays approval. 

{
  "operationRef": "operation-ref:v1:48619c00cce83a5cb797d3188cdf2225"
}
Binding field What it prevents
caseKey Reuse for a different recurring case
catalogHash Acting after the catalog changed
evidenceHash Evidence substitution
planHash Target, field, action, or value substitution
policyHash Reuse from another policy result
changeIntentHash Changing the exact Git change
attemptKey Reuse across attempts
Expiry and evidence age Stale authorization 
{
  "approved": true,
  "idempotencyKey": "attempt:v1:c6a3967e3d020c213af8697c45487dbf",
  "approvalBinding": {
    "caseKey": "case:v1:6b0fa0d60c77c1ac5a9202a01c05b4d3",
    "changeIntentHash": "change-intent:v1:724ed65eab3197d755832a61f894bad8",
    "remediationAttemptId": "attempt:v1:c6a3967e3d020c213af8697c45487dbf"
  },
  "operationRef": "operation-ref:v1:48619c00cce83a5cb797d3188cdf2225"
}

A committed manual decision is terminal. An identical retry replays it; an attempted reversal is rejected.

Step 5: provider preflight

5

Admit routes before any external write

The same handle resolves to approved context. The gateway evaluates sink routing and connectivity without creating a PR, ITSM record, or incident. 

{
  "operationRef": "operation-ref:v1:48619c00cce83a5cb797d3188cdf2225"
}
{
  "catalogEntryId": "mimir.compactor.pvc-pressure",
  "idempotencyKey": "attempt:v1:c6a3967e3d020c213af8697c45487dbf",
  "decision": "allow",
  "checks": [
    {
      "provider": "servicenow",
      "role": "itsm",
      "required": true,
      "status": "passed",
      "sideEffectCreated": false
    },
    {
      "provider": "pagerduty",
      "role": "incident",
      "required": true,
      "status": "passed",
      "sideEffectCreated": false
    }
  ],
  "violations": [],
  "directMutation": false,
  "operationRef": "operation-ref:v1:48619c00cce83a5cb797d3188cdf2225"
}

Preflight is admission evidence, not proof that a later write will succeed. Credentials can be revoked after preflight; the outbox still models partial and ambiguous outcomes.

Step 6: exact draft GitOps PR

6

Record intent, then publish exact Git objects

The handle resolves to approved state. The gateway prepares exact repository, base, tree, commit, branch, and diff coordinates, records the outbox intent, then calls Git.

Request:

{
  "operationRef": "operation-ref:v1:48619c00cce83a5cb797d3188cdf2225"
}

Approved change:

 compactor:
   persistentVolume:
-    size: 200Gi
+    size: 300Gi

The external-operation key is:

side-effect:v1:e9b366fc109fedbbf9507a3ff6c19ae6

The outbox record exists before the provider call. It binds the prepared proposal and progresses through fenced states such as pending, claimed, unknown external state, completed, failed, or dead letter.

Representative provider response for this fixture:

{
  "created": true,
  "directMutation": false,
  "provider": "github",
  "catalogEntryId": "mimir.compactor.pvc-pressure",
  "repository": "observability-gitops",
  "baseBranch": "main",
  "branch": "aiops/mimir-compactor-pvc-pressure-e9b366fc",
  "targetPath": "clusters/contextual-cellgroup-a/namespaces/mimir/mimir/values.yaml",
  "prNumber": 82,
  "requiresHumanApproval": true,
  "autoMerge": false,
  "allowsSelfMerge": false,
  "idempotencyKey": "side-effect:v1:e9b366fc109fedbbf9507a3ff6c19ae6",
  "operationRef": "operation-ref:v1:48619c00cce83a5cb797d3188cdf2225"
}

The gateway does not merge its own PR. Branch protection, ownership, review, and merge remain external controls.

Step 7: provider notifications

The fanout call carries only the handle:

{
  "operationRef": "operation-ref:v1:48619c00cce83a5cb797d3188cdf2225"
}

The gateway reloads the completed Git result, uses the preflight-admitted sink routes, derives one operation key per sink, and persists an outbox record before each write.

{
  "schemaVersion": "aiops.provider-notification-fanout.v1",
  "catalogEntryId": "mimir.compactor.pvc-pressure",
  "decision": "allow",
  "created": true,
  "directMutation": false,
  "dispatches": [
    {
      "role": "itsm",
      "provider": "servicenow",
      "correlation": "side-effect:v1:[service-now-operation]"
    },
    {
      "role": "incident",
      "provider": "pagerduty",
      "correlation": "side-effect:v1:[pager-duty-operation]"
    }
  ],
  "operationRef": "operation-ref:v1:48619c00cce83a5cb797d3188cdf2225"
}

Kestra may retry the HTTP task, but provider-attempt eligibility, backoff, exhaustion, and replay are decided from durable gateway state. A Kestra retry, gateway retry, and human replay therefore cannot multiply into unbounded provider writes.

Step 8: Argo read-only verification

{
  "operationRef": "operation-ref:v1:48619c00cce83a5cb797d3188cdf2225"
}

Before merge, the expected state is:

{
  "created": false,
  "directMutation": false,
  "prObserved": true,
  "verificationState": "awaiting_merge",
  "postMergeChecks": [
    "expected revision not yet deployed",
    "no direct Kubernetes mutation attempted"
  ],
  "operationRef": "operation-ref:v1:48619c00cce83a5cb797d3188cdf2225"
}

After a human merge, verification binds to the expected revision. Generic Synced and Healthy without the approved revision are insufficient. The gateway’s Argo/Kubernetes client is read-only.

Step 9: audit

The final request contains the handle plus non-authoritative audit metadata:

{
  "operationRef": "operation-ref:v1:48619c00cce83a5cb797d3188cdf2225",
  "orchestrator": "kestra",
  "flow": "aiops.kestra/catalog_remediation_aiops"
}

The gateway reconstructs the bundle from server-issued components, the attempt spine, outbox rows, provider readback, verification, and compensation records. It does not trust a client-provided claim that an operation succeeded.

4. Identity, exactly

Different identities answer different questions:

Identity Question Example
Alert fingerprint Which source episode or notification membership arrived? alert:v1:2cacc6298d7fd96fec6e4296e8dc5794
Remediation scope Which catalog target and material topology are affected? remediation:v1:ee5b4d4297b1242f6dfd9b33ec6217b4
Operational case Which recurring failure case is this? case:v1:6b0fa0d60c77c1ac5a9202a01c05b4d3
Audit lineage Which alert delivery is attached to the case? audit:v1:9c3cff8c38974c415bdad7075e4cbc7b
Change intent Which exact field/value change was proposed? change-intent:v1:724ed65eab3197d755832a61f894bad8
Attempt Which authorization attempt owns this intent? attempt:v1:c6a3967e3d020c213af8697c45487dbf
External operation Which provider action may execute? side-effect:v1:e9b366fc109fedbbf9507a3ff6c19ae6
Operation reference Which server snapshot should later routes load? operation-ref:v1:48619c00cce83a5cb797d3188cdf2225

Every public key uses:

versioned-prefix + first 32 hexadecimal characters of SHA-256(canonical JSON)

Canonical JSON recursively sorts object keys, uses compact separators, encodes UTF-8, and has no whitespace-dependent representation.

Identity 1: alert lineage

For a single instance, the source includes the native Grafana fingerprint. For a group, the gateway sorts member fingerprints so alert order cannot change the group identity.

{
  "schemaVersion": "aiops.alert-identity.v1",
  "fingerprints": ["grafana-labels-fingerprint-9d8c7b6a"]
}
alert:v1:2cacc6298d7fd96fec6e4296e8dc5794

Changing group membership changes alert and audit lineage. Reordering the same members does not.

Identity 2: remediation scope

The scope combines catalog meaning, target, and only declared material topology:

{
  "catalogEntryId": "mimir.compactor.pvc-pressure",
  "failureModeKey": "mimir.compactor.pvc-pressure",
  "target": {
    "repository": "observability-gitops",
    "pathTemplate": "clusters/{cluster}/namespaces/{namespace}/mimir/values.yaml",
    "field": "compactor.persistentVolume.size",
    "allowedFields": ["compactor.persistentVolume.size"]
  },
  "topologyScope": {
    "cellGroup": "contextual-cellgroup-a",
    "cluster": "contextual-cellgroup-a",
    "namespace": "mimir",
    "resource": "mimir-compactor-data"
  }
}

Identity 3: recurring operational case

This stable case answers: “is this the same cataloged failure against the same material target?” It excludes delivery order, model rationale wording, timestamps, and provider response text.

{
  "catalogEntryId": "mimir.compactor.pvc-pressure",
  "failureModeKey": "mimir.compactor.pvc-pressure",
  "schemaVersion": "aiops.identity.v1",
  "scopeLabels": {},
  "target": {
    "allowedFields": ["compactor.persistentVolume.size"],
    "field": "compactor.persistentVolume.size",
    "pathTemplate": "clusters/{cluster}/namespaces/{namespace}/mimir/values.yaml",
    "repository": "observability-gitops",
    "resource": "mimir-compactor"
  },
  "topologyScope": {
    "cellGroup": "contextual-cellgroup-a",
    "cluster": "contextual-cellgroup-a",
    "namespace": "mimir",
    "resource": "mimir-compactor-data"
  }
}
Full SHA-256:
6b0fa0d60c77c1ac5a9202a01c05b4d33e00c0e25766c477fcf74fdc2f18e800

Public key:
case:v1:6b0fa0d60c77c1ac5a9202a01c05b4d3

Grouped alerts and duplicate cases

Two Grafana groups can refer to the same remediation scope:

Property Same members, reordered Same scope, changed membership Material topology conflict
Alert fingerprint Same Different No identity issued
Audit lineage Same Different No identity issued
Case key Same Same No identity issued
Attempt/provider operation Same when exact intent is unchanged Same when exact intent is unchanged No action
Provider mutation Replay Replay Denied before action

If grouped alerts disagree on a field declared in identityTopology or scopeLabels, the gateway raises AmbiguousRemediationScopeError before identity issuance. It does not choose the first alert.

The managed-Kubernetes lineage rehearsal proved three payloads: two order-permuted alerts retained group/audit identity; adding a homogeneous member changed alert/audit lineage while retaining case, remediation scope, attempt, and provider authority; no duplicate provider mutation was created.

5. What Postgres owns

Table roles

Table Key Role
aiops_state_records (category, idempotency_key) Typed components and operation snapshots as JSONB
aiops_remediation_attempts attempt_key Immutable case/catalog binding and monotonic terminal outcome
aiops_outbox_records record_key External-operation state machine, claim fencing, retry eligibility
aiops_compensation_records Compensation key Durable work after a partial multi-system outcome
aiops_audit_records Audit lineage Latest audit projection
aiops_audit_events Audit lineage + sequence Append-only, hash-chained audit history
aiops_control_flags Flag name Runtime kill switch and control state

The attempt table no longer stores one mutable audit_case_key column. Audit projections and append-only events each carry generated attempt_key and audit_case_key columns from their JSON documents. Those child rows express the one-attempt-to-many-lineages relation without rebinding the immutable attempt.

Representative operation snapshot

{
  "category": "operationSnapshots",
  "idempotency_key": "operation-ref:v1:48619c00cce83a5cb797d3188cdf2225",
  "document": {
    "attemptKey": "attempt:v1:c6a3967e3d020c213af8697c45487dbf",
    "issuedComponentKey": "audit:v1:9c3cff8c38974c415bdad7075e4cbc7b",
    "ownerCallerId": "shared-secret",
    "completedRoutes": [
      "/policy",
      "/approval/request",
      "/providers/preflight",
      "/gitops/pr",
      "/providers/notify",
      "/argo/verify"
    ],
    "revision": 6
  }
}

The snapshot advances under an exclusive state-operation lock. Immutable components cannot be replaced. Only explicitly modeled transitions, such as pending approval becoming terminal, are allowed.

Representative outbox row

{
  "record_key": "side-effect:v1:e9b366fc109fedbbf9507a3ff6c19ae6",
  "attempt_key": "attempt:v1:c6a3967e3d020c213af8697c45487dbf",
  "operation": "gitops_pr",
  "status": "completed",
  "side_effect_id": "pr:82",
  "document": {
    "approvedProposalDigest": "[SHA-256]",
    "providerAttemptCount": 1,
    "providerReadbackConfirmed": true,
    "directMutation": false
  }
}

Postgres first-writer rules, compare-and-set transitions, leases, and unique keys decide which replica may perform an effect. Python coordinates the protocol; it does not rely on an in-memory distributed lock.

Representative audit event

{
  "audit_case_key": "audit:v1:9c3cff8c38974c415bdad7075e4cbc7b",
  "event_sequence": 4,
  "previous_event_hash": "[event 3 hash]",
  "event_hash": "[SHA-256 of canonical event 4]",
  "bundle_hash": "[SHA-256 of reconstructed audit bundle]"
}

Sequence is explicit and unique within a lineage. A missing, reordered, or changed event breaks chain verification.

Forensic queries

-- Attempt spine
select attempt_key, case_key, catalog_entry_id, terminal_status, document
from aiops_control_plane.aiops_remediation_attempts
where attempt_key = 'attempt:v1:c6a3967e3d020c213af8697c45487dbf';

-- Every alert/audit lineage attached to that attempt
select distinct attempt_key, audit_case_key
from aiops_control_plane.aiops_audit_events
where attempt_key = 'attempt:v1:c6a3967e3d020c213af8697c45487dbf'
order by audit_case_key;

-- Server-owned snapshot behind the handle
select category, idempotency_key, document
from aiops_control_plane.aiops_state_records
where category = 'operationSnapshots'
  and idempotency_key =
    'operation-ref:v1:48619c00cce83a5cb797d3188cdf2225';

-- External operations
select operation, operation_key, status, side_effect_id, updated_at, document
from aiops_control_plane.aiops_outbox_records
where attempt_key = 'attempt:v1:c6a3967e3d020c213af8697c45487dbf'
order by updated_at;

-- Tamper-evident audit history
select event_sequence, previous_event_hash, event_hash, bundle_hash, created_at
from aiops_control_plane.aiops_audit_events
where audit_case_key = 'audit:v1:9c3cff8c38974c415bdad7075e4cbc7b'
order by event_sequence;

6. Failure and recovery

Failure Expected behavior Forbidden behavior
Component supplied with operationRef Reject substitution Caller state replacing server state
Foreign caller uses handle Authorization-scope mismatch Cross-caller replay
Route skips prerequisite Missing-context rejection Out-of-order effect
Model unavailable or malformed Planning fails closed Implicit direct action
Policy deny Terminal audit, remediation skipped Approval/provider write
Stale approval binding Authorization fails Reuse against changed intent
Git failure before acceptance Bounded retry when eligible False success
Git accepts but response is lost Explicit ambiguity and exact readback Blind second create
Gateway dies after intent Another replica claims after lease expiry Two active claimants
Retry budget exhausts Dead letter and terminal replay Endless retry storm
Argo is healthy at wrong revision Verification fails Generic health treated as proof
Audit append fails Not declared complete Duplicate provider mutation

The system does not claim a distributed exactly-once transaction across Git, ITSM, incident management, and Kubernetes. It claims one durable operation authority, at-most-one admitted mutation within retention, exact correlation/readback, bounded retry, explicit unknown state, and a human-reviewed GitOps boundary.

Reconciliation sweep

GET /v1/reconciliation/sweep
X-AIOPS-Caller-Id: reconciliation-sweep
X-AIOPS-Gateway-Token: [redacted]

The sweep surfaces expired claims, failed or unknown outbox rows, compensation, dead letters, and backlog signals. It does not run PVC PromQL or select the Mimir catalog entry.

7. Executable assurance

Current evidence baseline

Gate Result
Python quality 231 passed, 1 skipped
Positive TLA+ models 21 models, 174 named invariants
Expected-failure TLA+ 76 of 76 reached named first violation
Mutation sentinels 80 of 80 killed
Postgres Testcontainers Passed, including audit-chain verification
Gitea exact GitOps Passed, including complete-diff rejection
Gitea crash recovery Passed at branch, commit, and PR response-loss windows
Catalog GitOps plus Argo 15 of 15 executable cases healthy
Local CTK gateway matrix 120 of 120
Local CTK provider matrix 17 of 17
Golden-path pressure 1,000 events passed
Managed Kubernetes rehearsals 3 of 3 
run:
simplify-invariant-kernel-final-20260715T064345Z

image:
sha256:3cf3f8d9cfdb97e0263a1a94e768a386389c6e4a08a3f0f40c2797f83180cf71

source:
15c475087813f8e6aadd57e63762291e74452d0736ed3acf94dbdbe7cfe8ce89
Managed case Evidence
Cross-replica response-loss recovery Distinct pod identities, one accepted object per provider, exact readback, no traffic on completed replay
Shared retry budget and dead letter One accepted mutation, five durable attempts, later readback only, terminal dead letter, zero post-terminal traffic
Grouped-alert lineage Order stable, changed membership changed audit lineage, same attempt/provider authority, no duplicate mutation

This is strong bounded evidence, not a claim that every enterprise tenant or future provider version has been proven.

8. Invariants

Invariant Enforcement
No direct production mutation Compiled catalog, OPA, typed plan, PR-only provider, read-only Argo
The model cannot authorize Policy, approval, credentials, and effect executor remain outside it
Caller cannot substitute state Post-policy calls carry operationRef; snapshot is server-owned
Caller cannot steal a handle Snapshot binds ownerCallerId
Routes cannot skip prerequisites Snapshot route requirements fail closed
Approval binds exact intent Catalog, evidence, plan, policy, target, value, and attempt digests
Durable intent precedes effect Outbox transition precedes provider mutation
One operation converges to one object Unique key, fenced claim, deterministic correlation, exact readback
Unknown is not success Accepted-write/lost-response remains ambiguous until readback
Human merge is the boundary Draft PR, branch protection, no self-merge
Healthy is revision-specific Argo checks expected revision
Audit derives from trusted state Durable components, outbox, readback, and hash chain
One attempt may own multiple alert lineages Separate attempt-to-audit-lineage relation

9. Operator walkthrough

  1. Open the Grafana rule/contact point and identify the Kestra webhook.
  2. Open a Kestra execution; show full bodies only in evidence, plan, and policy.
  3. Copy the operationRef from policy.
  4. Query aiops_state_records for its operation snapshot.
  5. Show completedRoutes and revision advancing.
  6. Query the attempt and its audit-lineage relation.
  7. Query every outbox row for the attempt.
  8. Compare the draft PR’s repository, base, branch, path, and diff with approval.
  9. Replay the webhook; verify the same authority and no extra provider object.
  10. Inspect the expected Argo revision and audit event chain.

10. Source map

Concern Repository source
Canonical workflow examples/kestra-aiops/flows/catalog_remediation_aiops.yml
Grafana fixture examples/kestra-aiops/fixtures/grafana-webhook-mimir-compactor.json
Reviewed catalog catalog/remediation-catalog.yaml
Catalog compiler examples/kestra-aiops/scripts/compile_remediation_catalog.py
Runtime catalog examples/kestra-aiops/planner-gateway/catalog/remediation-catalog.compiled.json
Request/response contracts aiops_control_plane/_request_contracts.py, contracts.py
HTTP/caller authentication aiops_control_plane/http_server.py
Operation references aiops_control_plane/operation_snapshots.py
Identity aiops_control_plane/identity.py, identity_context.py
Policy policies/aiops_policy.rego
Approval/authorization aiops_control_plane/workflow/approvals.py, authorization.py
Side-effect protocol aiops_control_plane/side_effects.py
Postgres/schema aiops_control_plane/postgres_repository.py, alembic/versions/
Git preparation/providers aiops_control_plane/git_proposal.py, providers/github.py, providers/_github/
Argo readback aiops_control_plane/providers/argo.py
Formal manifest aiops_control_plane/formal_contract.py

One-sentence description: Grafana detects a cataloged condition; Kestra sequences the protocol; the gateway turns evidence into one caller-, policy-, and approval-bound operation; Postgres makes external effects recoverable; Git and human review remain the mutation boundary; Argo and audit prove the result.
